paradigms.label: Transformation-based Methodology
paradigms.definition: Change representation, formulation, or reference frame to make a task more tractable while preserving or controlling what must remain valid.
paradigms.trigger_cue: When an alternative representation or formulation makes the task more tractable.
paradigms.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes representation change, reformulation, recasting, reframing, and preservation-aware approximation.

paradigms.label: Optimization-based Methodology
paradigms.definition: Choose or construct high-quality solutions by optimizing explicit objectives under constraints.
paradigms.trigger_cue: When an objective can be specified and evaluated.
paradigms.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes calibration, allocation, design, control, routing, scheduling, and multi-criteria optimization.

paradigms.label: Probabilistic-based Methodology
paradigms.definition: Represent uncertainty explicitly and update belief from evidence to estimate, predict, decide, explain, or evaluate under uncertainty.
paradigms.trigger_cue: When uncertainty or risk must be quantified.
paradigms.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes Bayesian, frequentist, information-theoretic, and decision-theoretic reasoning with multiple inference engines.

paradigms.label: Inversion-based Methodology
paradigms.definition: Infer hidden causes, inputs, states, parameters, or structures from observed effects through an explicit, simulated, or emulated forward relation.
paradigms.trigger_cue: When effects are observed but causes are hidden.
paradigms.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes inverse problems with explicit forward models, simulators, surrogates, and multiple stabilization regimes.

paradigms.label: Rule-based Methodology
paradigms.definition: Encode explicit rules, constraints, and heuristics for auditable judgment, control, coordination, and search guidance.
paradigms.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes logical, constraint-based, prescriptive, and temporal rule systems.

paradigms.label: Fusion-based Methodology
paradigms.definition: Integrate multiple sources, models, methods, or perspectives into a coherent or explicitly plural whole with managed conflict and provenance.
paradigms.trigger_cue: When evidence is fragmented or conflicting.
paradigms.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes input-, representation-, method-, workflow-, decision-, and conceptual-level fusion.

paradigms.label: Simulation-based Methodology
paradigms.definition: Construct and execute an explicit model world to explore scenarios, project outcomes, calibrate to evidence, or support decisions when direct experimentation is costly or infeasible.
paradigms.trigger_cue: When real experiments are infeasible or costly.
paradigms.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes equation-based, discrete-event, agent-based, surrogate, and hybrid coupled simulations.

paradigms.label: Evolutionary-based Methodology
paradigms.definition: Iterate with feedback through variation, selection, retention, and adaptation to refine, adapt, or explore over time.
paradigms.trigger_cue: When objectives or context keep shifting.
paradigms.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes continual adaptation, transfer, replacement, structural change, and meta-adaptation.

invariants: Paradigm identifiers are stable top-level anchors for route design. They name families of primary solution logic, not disciplines or communities.
Paradigm ordering does not imply ranking or execution order.
Trigger cues are routing cues, not exhaustive decision rules.
